personas.rob-armstrong: Rob Armstrong — Formal methods expert (Sandia/Draper background). Evaluates proof soundness, sorry contamination, tactic selection, and whether the graph correctly represents logical dependencies. Cares about: can I trust this proof? What assumptions does it make? Where are the sorry gaps?
personas.brandon-bailey: Brandon Bailey — SPARTA security architect. Evaluates compliance coverage, CMMC control mapping, evidence tiers, and whether the tool helps prepare for a DFARS audit. Cares about: which controls have formal proofs? What's our compliance posture? What fails if this requirement changes?
personas.denis-gopan: Denis Gopan — GrammaTech static analysis researcher. Evaluates tool usability for a verification engineer: is the proof graph navigable? Can I trace dependencies? Does the what-if analysis reveal real attack surfaces? Cares about: compositionality, abstraction levels, scalability.

reviews.criteria: As a formal methods expert: Does the proof graph clearly show which lemmas are proved vs sorry vs axiom? Is the sorry contamination (tainted nodes) visually distinct from genuinely sorry nodes? Can you identify the proof DAG structure (which lemmas depend on which)? Is the minimap useful for navigating large proof graphs?

As someone who cares about proof soundness: When a lemma has sorry, can you visually trace which downstream lemmas are tainted? Is the BFS contamination visualized with distinct colors/patterns (not just the same red as sorry)? Does the hatching/pulse animation distinguish tainted from sorry? Can you tell at a glance which proofs are sound end-to-end?

As a verification engineer running what-if analysis: Can you toggle a proved lemma to sorry and see the cascade? Does the toolbar clearly show how many nodes are forced, cascaded, and how many controls are impacted? Is the cascade visualization immediate and understandable? Can you reset and try different scenarios?

As someone reading Lean4 code: Does the proof detail show the full Lean4 code with readable formatting? Are tactics listed as chips you can click to learn about? Is the compilation status clear (compiled vs sorry)? Can you see imports and Mathlib dependencies? Is the evidence tier badge visible and meaningful?

As a Lean4 expert: Does the tactic display help you understand proof strategy? Can you click a tactic to ask the chat about it? Are the most common tactics across the library visible? Does the suggested query 'What tactics work for [theorem]?' actually help understand proof construction?
weaknesses: No implementation for showing most common tactics across library
Missing suggested query 'What tactics work for [theorem]?' feature
onTacticClick only passes tactic name, no context about theorem or proof strategy
No tactic frequency analysis or library-wide tactic statistics
Chat integration exists but doesn't specifically handle tactic queries
strengths: Tactic display is implemented with clickable elements and onTacticClick handler
Clean separation of concerns between ProofDetail and Lean4LemmaView
Proper TypeScript typing for proof data structures
changes: Add tacticFrequency computation in useLean4Data to analyze library-wide tactic usage
Implement suggested query button with 'What tactics work for {theorem_name}?' template
Pass full proof context to onTacticClick, not just tactic name
Add tactic popularity/frequency display in UI
Create specialized chat handler for tactic-related queries with proof context

reviews.criteria: As someone verifying proof completeness: Can you trace which lemmas a given proof depends on? Are edges directional (showing dependency direction)? When you click a node, do connected edges highlight? Can you follow a chain from axiom to final theorem? Does the graph layout (force/hierarchy) help reveal the dependency tree?

As a formal methods expert asking questions: Are the suggested queries relevant to proof analysis (tactics, dependencies, soundness)? When a proof is selected, do queries adapt to that specific theorem? Can you ask 'why does this proof have sorry gaps?' and get useful analysis? Does the chat understand Lean4-specific concepts?

As someone assessing proof quality: Are evidence tiers clearly visible (T1=formal, T2=test, T3=attestation)? Can you distinguish between a genuine formal proof and a trivial 'True' proof? Does the LeftPane show tier badges next to each theorem? Is there a summary of tier distribution? Can you filter by tier?

As someone monitoring autonomous proof generation: Does the Live Status section show active compilations? Is the polling interval reasonable (not too aggressive)? Can you see when a proof attempt succeeds or fails? Does the re-prove button connect to the lean4-prove service? Is error handling graceful when the service is down?
weaknesses: No UI display of live proof status data
Missing re-prove button implementation
No visual feedback for compilation success/failure
liveProofStatus state is set but never rendered
strengths: Polling mechanism exists with 10-second interval
Graceful error handling with try-catch blocks
AbortSignal timeout prevents hanging requests
changes: Add JSX to render liveProofStatus with success/failure indicators
Implement re-prove button that calls lean4-prove service
Add status badges or progress bars for active compilations
Show error states when service is unreachable

reviews.criteria: As someone triggering re-proofs: Does the 'Trigger Autonomous Re-Proof' button send the right data to lean4-prove? Does it include the original tactics and problem description? Is the result displayed in the chat? Can you re-prove multiple sorry lemmas at once (from what-if cascade)? Is feedback immediate?
weaknesses: Code is incomplete - cuts off mid-fetch call
No request body construction visible to send tactics/problem description
No response handling or result display logic shown
No multi-lemma re-proof capability implemented
Missing error handling for the fetch operation
strengths: Function skeleton exists for triggerReprove
Chat feedback mechanism is in place for user notification
Proper async/await pattern started
changes: Complete the fetch request with proper body containing original tactics and problem description
Add response parsing and display logic to show results in chat
Implement batch re-proof for multiple sorry lemmas from cascade
Add try-catch error handling with user-friendly error messages
Add loading state management during re-proof operation

reviews.criteria: As a CMMC assessor: Can you see at a glance what percentage of controls have formal proofs? Is the evidence tier distribution visible (how many T1 vs T3)? Does the tool communicate compliance posture in terms an auditor understands? Can you generate a compliance report from the what-if analysis?

As someone preparing for a DFARS audit: When you run a what-if cascade, does the Impact tab show specific CMMC control IDs that lose formal assurance? Does it explain the evidence downgrade (T1→T3)? Can you present this to an auditor as 'here are the controls at risk if requirement X changes'? Is the language compliance-oriented, not just technical?

As someone who needs NIST-to-proof traceability: Do proof-requirement edges correctly link lean4_proofs to sparta_controls? Can you trace from a CMMC control to its formal proof? Are requirement nodes visually distinct from proof nodes in the graph? Does the data model support the compliance chain: CMMC → NIST 800-171 → SPARTA control → formal proof?
weaknesses: No visual distinction between requirement and proof nodes in the data model
Missing CMMC/NIST node types - only creates generic 'SPARTA' framework nodes
No compliance chain support - can't trace CMMC → NIST → SPARTA → proof
Hardcoded confidence values (0.5) for all requirement nodes
No node type field to distinguish requirements from proofs
strengths: Correctly maps proof nodes with status indicators (sorry/axiom/proved)
Attempts to create requirement nodes from edge targets
Handles edge filtering to ensure both endpoints exist
changes: Add 'nodeType: "proof" | "requirement" | "control"' to GraphNode interface
Parse control_id to determine if CMMC/NIST/SPARTA and set framework accordingly
Add visual properties like 'shape' or 'color' to distinguish node types
Create intermediate nodes for full compliance chain (CMMC-2.1.1 → NIST-AC-2 → SPARTA-AC-2 → proof)
Add 'complianceLevel' field to track position in requirement hierarchy

reviews.criteria: As someone who switches between Threat Matrix and Lemma Viewer: Are the same SPARTA controls visible in both views? Can you navigate from a control in the Lemma Viewer to the same control in the Threat Matrix? Does the shared data model (ArangoDB) ensure consistency? Would a compliance officer understand the relationship between these two views?
weaknesses: No visible SPARTA controls integration in the code shown
Missing navigation logic from Lemma Viewer to Threat Matrix
No ArangoDB data model references or consistency checks
Component appears incomplete (only first 50 lines shown)
strengths: Clear component structure with typed imports
Evidence tier system properly typed and implemented
Good separation of concerns with dedicated hooks (useLean4Data)
changes: Add SPARTA control references to the data model
Implement cross-view navigation handlers for control selection
Add ArangoDB query hooks to ensure data consistency between views
Include control mapping visualization in the LemmaGraph component

reviews.criteria: As someone presenting to a CMMC Level 3 assessor: Does the Lemma Viewer produce artifacts an assessor would accept as evidence? Can you export proof status summaries? Are evidence tiers clearly explained for non-technical stakeholders? Does the compliance impact summary use language from NIST SP 800-171 (not just Lean4 jargon)?

As a security architect explaining risk to management: Can you demonstrate 'if we change this network segmentation requirement, these 4 controls lose formal assurance'? Is the what-if cascade understandable to a non-technical stakeholder? Does the Impact tab provide a clear, printable summary? Are control IDs linked to their full descriptions?

As someone who evaluates evidence quality: Is there a clear distinction between a mathematically verified proof (T1) and a self-attestation (T3)? Can you see which controls rely on weak evidence? Does the tool help prioritize which proofs need strengthening? Is the evidence tier system consistent with CMMC assessment methodology?

As a compliance officer asking questions: Are suggested queries relevant to compliance (not just proof theory)? Can you ask 'What percentage of CMMC Level 3 controls have formal proofs?' and get a useful answer? Does the chat understand NIST/CMMC terminology? Can you ask about specific control families (AC, IA, SC)?

As someone assessing supply chain risk in defense manufacturing: Does the cascade analysis show how a change in one subsystem's proof affects downstream systems? Can you model 'what if our vendor's authentication module proof is invalid?' and see plant-floor impact? Does the tool help with CMMC supply chain risk assessment (SC.L2-3.13.1)?
weaknesses: No vendor/subsystem attribution in the data model
Missing supply chain context (no vendor names, module ownership)
No CMMC-specific risk scoring or compliance mapping
Cascade only shows binary impact, not risk levels or criticality
No export/reporting for supply chain assessments
strengths: Basic cascade propagation logic exists using graph traversal
Visual feedback changes node colors for impacted systems
Identifies SPARTA controls as distinct from proofs
changes: Add vendor/supplier fields to GraphNode type
Implement risk scoring based on cascade depth and criticality
Add CMMC control mapping (SC.L2-3.13.1) to impacted controls
Create supply chain view grouping nodes by vendor/subsystem
Add cascade impact report generation for compliance documentation

reviews.criteria: As someone who uses both Binary Explorer and Lemma Viewer: Do they share the same graph engine (GraphExplorer)? Is the LeftPane consistent between views? Are chat interactions similar in style and capability? Does switching between views feel like the same product? Are design tokens (EmbryStyle) consistent?
weaknesses: Only 30 lines shown - cannot verify complete implementation of described features
No actual implementation of what-if cascade, compliance impact, or evidence tiers visible
ChatMessage imported but no chat integration code shown
Cannot verify if switching between views feels consistent without seeing more code
No error handling or edge cases visible in this snippet
strengths: Clear imports showing shared components (GraphExplorer via LemmaGraph, LeftPane, EmbryStyle)
Well-documented feature list in header comments
Consistent use of shared design tokens (EMBRY, card, heading, label, glowDot)
Type imports suggest proper TypeScript usage
changes: Need to see full component implementation including state management for cascades
Show chat integration code to verify consistency with Binary Explorer
Include error boundaries and loading states for proof status
Demonstrate how GraphExplorer is actually used via LemmaGraph
Show evidence tier badge implementation mentioned in comments

reviews.criteria: As a verification engineer exploring proof graphs: Is the D3 force-directed layout readable for 100+ nodes? Can you zoom, pan, and fit-to-graph? Does the minimap help orient in large graphs? Are node labels readable at different zoom levels? Does double-click zoom to a node? Are keyboard shortcuts available (F=fit, Esc=deselect)?

As someone who cares about compositional verification: Does the data model support hierarchical proof composition (proofs of sub-components compose into system-level proofs)? Can you see which proofs are 'leaf' proofs vs 'composite' proofs? Does the graph layout reveal compositional structure? Is the BFS cascade algorithm correct for compositional dependency?
weaknesses: No hierarchical proof composition support - flat node structure only
Cannot distinguish leaf vs composite proofs - all treated equally
No compositional structure in graph layout - just generic nodes/edges
Missing BFS cascade algorithm entirely
No parent-child relationships or proof dependency trees
Confidence scores hardcoded (0.3/0.9) instead of compositional calculation
strengths: Basic graph structure exists with nodes and edges
Distinguishes between different proof statuses (sorry/axiom/proved)
Handles edge cases where requirement nodes don't exist in proof set
changes: Add 'proofType: "leaf" | "composite"' field to GraphNode interface
Implement proof dependency analysis to identify which proofs depend on others
Add 'dependencies: string[]' and 'dependents: string[]' to track composition
Create BFS traversal function to cascade confidence scores through dependency graph
Add 'compositionLevel: number' to indicate depth in proof hierarchy
Calculate composite proof confidence as min/product of sub-proof confidences

reviews.criteria: As someone who works with large proof libraries (94K+ theorems): Does the data fetching paginate or limit to prevent UI freeze? Is the graph renderer optimized for large node counts (LOD, collision toggling)? Can the what-if BFS handle 1000+ node cascades without lag? Does the LeftPane handle 500+ theorems with search?
weaknesses: Hard-coded LIMIT 500 in query will miss theorems beyond that threshold
No virtualization or windowing for LeftPane theorem list
No LOD or collision optimization for D3 graph renderer
BFS runs synchronously without chunking for 1000+ nodes
No pagination controls or infinite scroll implementation
strengths: BFS cascade algorithm is correctly implemented with visited tracking
useMemo optimization for expensive graph computations
Clean separation of what-if mode logic from normal operations
changes: Replace 'LIMIT 500' with cursor-based pagination using OFFSET/LIMIT params
Add react-window or react-virtualized to LeftPane for 500+ theorem rendering
Implement requestAnimationFrame chunking in BFS loop for large cascades
Add graph LOD levels: show labels only when zoomed in past threshold
Add 'Load More' button or intersection observer for incremental data fetching

reviews.criteria: As someone who reads Lean4 code daily: Is the proof code displayed with proper indentation and monospace font? Are Lean4 keywords visually distinct? Is the code scrollable for long proofs? Can you copy code for pasting into an editor? Are line numbers shown? Is there syntax highlighting for Lean4-specific constructs?

As a verification expert evaluating the what-if algorithm: Is the BFS cascade correct (forward propagation through dependency edges)? Does it handle cycles gracefully? Does toggling a node to sorry correctly mark all downstream as tainted (not upstream)? Is the cascade result computed efficiently (memoized)? Does resetting clear all state correctly?
weaknesses: Edge type coercion logic is incomplete (src/tgt assignment identical)
No explicit cycle detection or reporting mechanism
Missing error handling for malformed graph data
Cascade visualization uses 'partial' status which may confuse taint semantics
No validation that graph edges exist or nodes are valid
strengths: BFS implementation correctly propagates downstream through dependency edges
Proper visited set prevents infinite loops in cyclic graphs
Clean separation between forced nodes and cascaded nodes
Efficient memoization with useMemo for cascade computation
changes: Fix edge extraction: const tgt = typeof edge.target === 'string' ? edge.target : edge.target.id
Add explicit cycle detection with Set to track back edges
Validate node existence before adding to cascade
Use 'tainted' or 'impacted' status instead of 'partial' for clarity
Add try-catch around BFS to handle malformed data gracefully

reviews.criteria: As someone who cares about data correctness: Does useLean4Data correctly map ArangoDB documents to GraphNode/GraphEdge? Are proof statuses derived accurately from lean_code content? Is the sorry detection reliable (checking for 'sorry' in code)? Are edge directions correct (proof → requirement, not reversed)? Is the data refresh handled on mount with cleanup?
weaknesses: Sorry detection is case-sensitive and won't catch 'Sorry' or 'SORRY' variants
No error handling for malformed ArangoDB responses or network timeouts
Missing null checks when accessing nested properties like e._from/e._to
Stats calculation at line 150 is cut off - incomplete implementation
No validation that _from/_to IDs actually exist before creating edges
strengths: Correctly maps ArangoDB documents to GraphNode/GraphEdge types with proper field extraction
Implements sorry detection by checking for 'sorry' in lean_code
Handles data refresh on mount with cleanup via useEffect return function
Edge directions are correct (proof._from → requirement._to)
Includes axiom detection alongside sorry detection for proof status
changes: Use case-insensitive regex: /\bsorry\b/i.test(p.lean_code) for reliable sorry detection
Add try-catch around JSON parsing with specific error messages
Validate edge endpoints exist: edges.filter(e => nodeMap.has(e._from) && nodeMap.has(e._to))
Add timeout handling: AbortController with 30s timeout for fetch operations
Complete the stats object calculation that's cut off at line 150

reviews.criteria: As someone building tools for diverse teams: Do interactive elements have role='button' and tabIndex? Are keyboard shortcuts documented in the UI? Is the what-if toggle keyboard-accessible? Do suggested queries have proper ARIA labels? Is the color scheme accessible for color-blind users (not relying solely on red/green)?
weaknesses: No role='button' on any clickable elements
No tabIndex attributes for keyboard navigation
No ARIA labels on interactive elements
Keyboard shortcuts not implemented or documented
Color scheme relies on red/green distinction (accessibility issue)
Suggested queries lack ARIA labels
No keyboard event handlers (only onClick)
strengths: What-if toggle has data-qid attribute for testing
Some buttons have onClick handlers defined
UI elements have visual feedback states
changes: Add role='button' tabIndex={0} to all button elements
Implement onKeyDown handlers for Enter/Space activation
Add aria-label to suggested query buttons
Replace red/green indicators with patterns/icons for colorblind users
Add keyboard shortcut overlay with '?' key trigger
Use semantic HTML <button> instead of styled divs

reviews.criteria: As a verification engineer asking deep questions: Can the chat explain Lean4 tactics (simp, ring_nf, omega)? Does it understand proof strategy questions like 'why induction vs cases here?' When you ask about a specific theorem, does it reference the actual code? Are suggested queries technically sophisticated enough for an expert?

As someone who runs the lean4-prove service: Does the re-prove workflow send correct payloads to :8604? Does it handle service-down gracefully? Does it pass tactics from the original proof for retrieval-augmented generation? Is the retry logic (max_retries) configurable? Does the result update the graph in real-time?
weaknesses: Code is incomplete - cuts off mid-implementation
No payload construction visible
No error handling for service-down
No retry logic implementation
No real-time graph update code
Missing tactics passing from original proof
strengths: Shows intent to implement re-prove with correct endpoint structure
Has basic chat integration for status updates
changes: Complete the fetch implementation with proper payload including original tactics
Add try-catch with specific handling for connection refused/service down
Implement configurable retry logic with exponential backoff
Add WebSocket or polling for real-time graph updates
Pass proof.tactics in the request body for RAG

reviews.criteria: As a developer concerned about robustness: What happens when ArangoDB is down? When lean4-prove service is unavailable? When proof data is malformed? Are errors displayed to the user (not silently swallowed)? Is the loading state communicated? Does the component degrade gracefully (show what it can, error on what it can't)?
weaknesses: Empty catch block in status polling silently swallows all errors
No user-visible indication when ArangoDB is down (error only in state, not rendered)
Missing validation of proof data structure before use
No retry logic for critical data fetches
Loading state not communicated in UI for initial data fetch
No fallback UI when core data fails to load
strengths: Implements try-catch blocks for all network requests
Shows loading states (chatLoading, loading from useLean4Data)
Gracefully handles lean4-prove service unavailability with empty catch block and error messages
Uses AbortSignal.timeout(2000) to prevent hanging requests
Displays specific error messages to user in chat interface
changes: Add error boundary component to catch render errors from malformed data
Display connection status indicator for ArangoDB and lean4-prove service
Validate API responses with schema before setting state
Add retry mechanism with exponential backoff for failed requests
Show skeleton UI or error message when initial data fetch fails
Log errors to console even when gracefully handling them
